flags.message: The provisioned snapshot for this petition contains only a bare SCOTUS docket label and sparse metadata: no party caption, docket entries, filing date, originating court, case type, or legal issue text. The prediction is therefore base-rate driven and low-confidence.

Corpus era labels are unreliable: `fedcourts query --court scotus --era 1990s` returns priors whose content is 19th-century state-court material carrying modern-looking docket numbers (e.g. 96-7185, 93-528) labeled era 1990s. The era derivation appears to misparse docket numbers/citations, making era-restricted retrieval unusable for period-matched priors.

Snapshot for scotus/1057937 is minimal: the caption is just the docket number ('No. 92-7301'), with no parties, lower court, dates, or docket entries. The only case-specific signal is the docket-number convention (OT1992 IFP series), so the prediction is necessarily a base-rate call.

The committed metrics/statpack.md lacks the 'by era' and 'Modern discretionary-cert petitions by disposition' sections that .github/prompts/predict.md instructs predictors to anchor on; the prompt and the statpack artifact are out of step.
The snapshot for this case contains no docket entries or substantive facts, making individualized prediction impossible. Falling back to base rates.

The provisioned snapshot is unusually sparse for a SCOTUS petition-disposition prediction: it lacks docket number, docket entries, filed/termination dates, cert grant/denial dates, originating court, issue/topic, and party detail beyond the caption. The prediction is therefore broad-prior driven.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare bulk-import docket: no docket entries, no dates, no docket number, no originating-court information, and no petition documents. The only case-specific signals are the caption 'Mason v. Rollins' and one opinion cluster (88494), which points to a nineteenth-century merits case decided in the writ-of-error era — there was no discretionary petition stage to predict. The prediction rests on corpus base rates for this population of sparse opinion-import SCOTUS dockets rather than on any petition-stage facts.

The provisioned snapshot is sparse for a petition-disposition prediction: it has no docket number, docket entries, filing or termination dates, cert-grant or cert-denial dates, originating court, issue statement, petition documents, or party roles beyond the case caption. The prediction therefore relies mainly on the caption, one cluster reference, and aggregate corpus base rates.

Snapshot is a near-empty docket shell: no docket entries, no docket number, no filed/argued/terminated or cert-stage dates, no panel, no originating court. The only substantive signals are the caption (United States v. Hodge) and one linked opinion cluster.

The petition framing is anachronistic for this docket: the caption and very low opinion-cluster id indicate an antebellum-era case that reached the Court on writ of error (mandatory review), so there was no certiorari petition to grant or deny. The prediction therefore turns mostly on how the reconciler labels historical merits cases (the corpus's resolved SCOTUS labels are ~78% 'other'). Consider whether petition events should be opened for pre-certiorari-era dockets.

Backtest leakage: the caption matches decided historical cases within the model's training knowledge (United States v. Hodge, 44 U.S. 534 (1845) and 47 U.S. 279 (1848)), though the snapshot lacks the detail to pin down which. For historical cells this measures recall plus calibration, not ex-ante forecasting skill.

The event asks for a petition disposition, but the provisioned snapshot has no docket entries, docket number, filed or termination dates, cert grant or denial dates, parties, originating court, or petition-specific metadata. The prediction is therefore calibrated mainly from corpus base rates and the sparse fact that an opinion cluster is linked.

Pre-1925 mandatory-jurisdiction case (Hopt v. Territory of Utah, 1880s writ of error) reached predict scope. The is_historical_mandatory exclusion needs a bare sequential docket number or a pre-1925 date_filed, but this corpus row has an empty docket_number and null dates, so it fell through; is_published_opinion_unresolvable also missed it because the row carries no citations/citation_count/opinion_text even though the docket snapshot links an opinion cluster (clusters/91057). A row-is-completely-bare-but-has-a-linked-cluster signal (or ingesting the cluster's citation data during pull) would catch this class.

Snapshot has no docket entries, no docket number, and all-null activity dates; the only substantive signal is the linked opinion cluster. The prediction is therefore driven almost entirely by era inference from the party name (Territory of Utah) and corpus base rates.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare SCOTUS docket stub: it has zero docket entries and no filing date, termination date, originating court, parties, issue description, or cert disposition fields. The prediction was completed from the sparse snapshot and corpus base-rate context, but the case-specific evidentiary basis is very limited.

Snapshot is maximally sparse: the caption is only the docket number ('No. 90-1397'), with no parties, no docket-number field, no dates, and no docket entries — only a link to one opinion cluster. Unlike sparse dockets with a recognizable caption, the underlying case cannot be identified, so the prediction is purely base-rate driven from corpus stats. If the linked cluster's disposition text were ingested into the corpus/snapshot, this docket would likely be recoverable (probe-recoverability candidate).

The snapshot is materially sparse for a SCOTUS petition-disposition prediction: it has no docket entries, docket number, lower-court metadata, cert-stage dates, question presented, parties, or petition text. It does include a linked opinion cluster, which may indicate an opinion-based historical record rather than a clean petition-order record. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate calibrated with low confidence.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare bulk-import docket: no docket entries, no dates, no docket number, and no petition documents. The only case-specific signals are the caption 'Bryan v. United States' and one opinion cluster — and the caption matches at least two distinct SCOTUS merits cases (338 U.S. 552 (1950) and 524 U.S. 184 (1998)), which the snapshot cannot disambiguate. The prediction rests on the observation that both candidates resolve identically (certiorari granted, judgment affirmed against the petitioner) plus corpus base rates.

The snapshot is a bare SCOTUS record: no docket number, docket entries, lower-court metadata, filing dates, cert dates, parties, attorneys, or petition text. It includes a linked cluster, which may indicate a published-opinion record rather than a clean modern discretionary-cert petition. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate calibrated.

The snapshot is a bare bulk-scrape docket stub: no docket entries, no docket number, no dates, null cert-granted/cert-denied fields, with one linked opinion cluster. This is the profile of a pre-certiorari-era (mandatory jurisdiction) SCOTUS case, so a petition-disposition event does not map cleanly onto it — it looks like the population the predict scope's pre-1925 exclusion targets, but the docket's missing dates likely prevented the exclusion predicate from classifying it. Consider a recoverability probe or latching the scope exclusion for this docket.

The snapshot is a bare SCOTUS stub: no docket number, docket entries, lower-court metadata, filing dates, cert dates, parties, or petition text. It includes a linked cluster, which may indicate a published-opinion record rather than a clean modern discretionary-cert petition. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate calibrated.

This looks like a #363-class case (historical SCOTUS docket, published opinion linked, outcome only in opinion text) that escaped the is_published_opinion_unresolvable exclusion: the docket snapshot carries a cluster link, but the corpus row's citations/citation_count/opinion_text are all empty, so the predicate cannot see the opinion. The empty docket number also defeats the #309 and #333 predicates. A cluster-detail backfill (or extending the #363 signal to the docket's cluster link) would take this class out of predict scope at the source.

Snapshot is metadata-only: empty docket number, no docket entries, no parties/attorneys, every activity date null. The prediction rests on the linked-opinion-cluster signal plus corpus base rates (resolved SCOTUS events are 78% 'other'), not on any petition-stage facts.
Snapshot contains no docket entries, dates, or lower court info. It is a stub for 'Minor v. Tillotson'. Cannot make an informed prediction; falling back to base rate.

The snapshot is a sparse SCOTUS docket with no docket number, entries, or cert dates, but it has a linked opinion cluster. Repository scope notes describe this historical/opinion-only shape as a mismatch for the modern discretionary-cert event model, so the prediction was completed conservatively as `other`.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare bulk-import docket: no docket entries, no dates, no docket number, and no petition documents. The only case-specific signal is the caption and one opinion cluster. The prediction therefore rests on recognizing the caption as the historical writ-of-error case Minor v. Tillotson (Louisiana land-title litigation, reported at 32 U.S. 99 (1833) and again in the 1840s), plus corpus base rates; a predictor without background knowledge of this case could only emit the raw base rate.

This case predates the certiorari system, so a petition-disposition event has no natural granted/denied reading; the disposition enum lacks affirmed/reversed labels, and corpus stats show ~78% of resolved SCOTUS events reconcile to 'other'. Choosing between 'other' and 'denied' is mostly a guess about the reconciler's label mapping rather than legal uncertainty. A documented mapping for historical merits dispositions would make these events crisper.

exclusions.reason: SCOTUS application / original-jurisdiction docket — not discretionary cert (#362)
SCOTUS disbarment docket — attorney discipline, not discretionary cert
bare bulk-import row whose snapshot links a published opinion cluster (#438)
consolidated docket whose members all classify out of scope (#449)
internally inconsistent dates — decided before filed (#171)
published opinion but no machine-readable cert disposition (#363)

unclassified.reason: docket Term not parseable (a format the predicate skips)
carries a disposition signal (open despite a recorded decision)

gaps.label: A documented disposition-label mapping for historical merits cases (affirmed/reversed -> other?) to remove label-choice guesswork
The provisioned snapshot exposed only a cluster URL, not the cluster metadata or petition-stage order text.
a derived era/Term field on the case row so historical cases can be base-rated against their own period
fedcourts query rows mostly lack case_name/date_filed/topic, which makes it hard to judge whether a prior is actually similar
no way to filter stats/query to cert-stage petition outcomes specifically; the resolved-label mix ('other'-dominated) is hard to interpret for a petition event
semantic/topic retrieval over historical SCOTUS priors would beat the structured filters here (no topic, judges, or citations on this stub docket to filter by)
surfacing the linked opinion cluster's non-outcome metadata (decision date, reporter citation) in the snapshot when the docket itself is bare
A --docket-number-class or motion-type filter (e.g. SCOTUS M-docket vs. cert docket) for query/stats; the M-docket population has a very different base rate than the cert docket
A --term or date cut was unusable because the snapshot has no filing date to anchor it
A SCOTUS petition base-rate view separated from historical opinion-cluster records would make calibration more reliable.

helpful.label: Schema files made the artifact contract clear.
fedcourts paths resolved the cell's I/O locations instantly
fedcourts paths resolved the event location
fedcourts paths resolved the event path.
fedcourts paths: confirmed resolved output locations
fedcourts stats gave SCOTUS disposition base rates

gaps: The provisioned snapshot lacked party names, docket entries, filing dates, originating court, case type, and legal issue text.
The committed statpack available in this checkout did not expose a named modern discretionary-cert cut in the rendered Markdown or machine JSON.
helpful: The path resolver identified the event and snapshot locations.
The committed statpack provided broad SCOTUS petition base-rate context.
recent.notes: I avoided broad corpus retrieval because the snapshot was a bare docket identifier and a generic SCOTUS query risked surfacing the same case's resolved outcome.

gaps: The 'Modern discretionary-cert petitions by disposition' and 'by era' statpack sections that predict.md tells predictors to anchor on are absent from the committed metrics/statpack.md.
A query filter on docket-number range/prefix (paid vs IFP series, e.g. 92-5001+) would give the one cut that actually matters for sparse Term-numbered SCOTUS dockets.
Era-restricted retrieval is currently unusable: --era 1990s surfaces 19th-century material with misparsed modern-looking docket numbers.
helpful: statpack per-Term table — showed early-1990s resolved labels are too thin to lean on, which shaped calibration.
fedcourts paths — quick confirmation of resolved input/output locations.
Prior committed predictor runs under data/ as a formatting and calibration reference.
recent.notes: Snapshot carried no caption, parties, or entries, so the prediction is a base-rate exercise; the docket-number convention (92-7301 = OT1992 IFP series) did all the case-specific work. Corpus query returned no usable priors for this era.

gaps: No substantive case facts were present in the snapshot.
recent.notes: Relied on structural base-rate knowledge due to empty docket snapshot.

gaps: A case-fact summary or normalized petition metadata in the provisioned snapshot would materially improve SCOTUS petition forecasts for sparse records.
helpful: fedcourts paths resolved the event and snapshot locations.
fedcourts stats provided SCOTUS disposition base-rate calibration.
recent.notes: The local query tool was available, but the target snapshot lacked the structured fields needed for a narrow similar-case cut.

gaps: A way to see the snapshot's associated opinion-cluster metadata (date, reporter citation) without fetching new case facts — it would disambiguate historical captions cheaply
Docket-level filter for fedcourts query (e.g. by case_id prefix or source) to find priors from the same bulk-import population directly
helpful: fedcourts stats --court scotus for the resolved-label distribution (other/dismissed/denied/granted), the main calibration anchor for this sparse docket
fedcourts query --disposition granted/dismissed spot-checks to see what the rare non-'other' labels actually attach to
recent.notes: Snapshot had no petition-stage facts at all (bulk-import shell), so the prediction is essentially base-rate-driven; see flags.json. Term-year and originating-court group-bys were mostly empty for the resolved SCOTUS set, limiting the recommended calibration cuts.

gaps: The snapshot lacks petition metadata such as docket number, docket entries, filing and termination dates, cert dates, lower-court source, issue statement, petition documents, and party roles.
A SCOTUS petition base-rate view separated from historical opinion-cluster records would make calibration more reliable.
helpful: fedcourts paths resolved the event location
fedcourts stats gave SCOTUS disposition base rates
fedcourts query gave qualitative examples of sparse SCOTUS disposition labels
recent.notes: The corpus tools were useful for base-rate calibration. The prediction remains low-confidence because the provisioned snapshot contains only sparse docket metadata and one cluster reference.

gaps: term_year grouping returns no populated buckets for historical SCOTUS dockets (no filed dates in the corpus), so no era-specific base rate was available
no way to see how the reconciler maps a historical merits opinion onto the petition-disposition label set; the resolved-label mix ('other'-dominated) had to stand in for that mapping
a per-shape base rate (e.g. dockets with a linked opinion cluster but no cert-stage dates) would answer this cell's main question directly
helpful: fedcourts paths resolved the cell's I/O locations instantly
fedcourts stats gave the resolved-label distribution, which was the decisive calibration input for this near-empty snapshot
fedcourts query confirmed the resolved SCOTUS priors share this docket's shape (historical shells resolving overwhelmingly to 'other')
recent.notes: The snapshot carried almost no case-specific signal, so this cell was decided by corpus base rates plus the era inference from the caption and low cluster id. The prior claude-baseline run on a similar historical shell (scotus/101968) was a useful template for the label-mapping uncertainty.

gaps: A normalized historical proceeding type or cluster disposition excerpt would make sparse nineteenth-century SCOTUS records easier to classify without guessing from metadata shape.
helpful: The provisioned snapshot made the permitted factual record clear.
fedcourts stats provided a useful SCOTUS base-rate anchor for calibration.
recent.notes: Used local corpus tooling only for priors and aggregate calibration, not for new facts about this case.
